brief_document.title: Implement Rust SDK core API: channels, conversations, and type enforcement
brief_document.purpose: Deliver the core application-facing API surface of the Rust SDK: ChannelHandle for typed publish/subscribe/request-reply (ADR-002), ConversationHandle for supervised message exchange (ADR-001), and the SchemaValidate trait for compile-time type enforcement. These types define the contract every deployment mode (embedded, remote) must satisfy.
brief_document.task: Create channel.rs, conversation.rs, and types.rs in crates/agora-sdk/src/. ChannelHandle is a trait with generic methods constrained by Serialize+SchemaValidate (publish) and Deserialize (subscribe). ConversationHandle is a trait with send/receive/lifecycle observation. SchemaValidate is a trait that message types implement to provide schema metadata for publish-time validation. All types are trait definitions at this stage -- concrete implementations come in SDK-004 (embedded/remote modes). IN SCOPE: trait definitions, type constraints, associated types, error integration with SdkError. OUT OF SCOPE: concrete implementations, connection management, backpressure types, embedded/remote distinction.

requirements.title: Define ChannelHandle trait with typed publish, subscribe, and request-reply
requirements.spec: WHEN a caller invokes publish on a ChannelHandle, THE SYSTEM SHALL require the message type to implement Serialize + SchemaValidate. WHEN a caller invokes subscribe, THE SYSTEM SHALL return a stream of messages whose type implements Deserialize. WHEN a caller invokes request_reply, THE SYSTEM SHALL send a message and return a future resolving to the reply. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT expose protocol framing or wire details through the ChannelHandle interface.
acceptance: ChannelHandle::publish<M: Serialize + SchemaValidate> compiles and accepts a typed message
ChannelHandle::subscribe returns an impl Stream<Item = Result<M, SdkError>> where M: Deserialize
ChannelHandle::request_reply<Req, Resp> sends Req and returns Future<Output = Result<Resp, SdkError>>
Attempting to publish a type not implementing SchemaValidate produces a compile-time error
ChannelHandle is object-safe or documents why it is not and provides an alternative

requirements.title: Define ConversationHandle trait with send, receive, and lifecycle observation
requirements.spec: WHEN a caller invokes send on a ConversationHandle, THE SYSTEM SHALL transmit a typed message within the conversation context. WHEN a caller invokes receive, THE SYSTEM SHALL return the next message in the conversation. THE SYSTEM SHALL provide lifecycle observation (opened, message, closing, closed, error) as a stream of ConversationEvent variants. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT require callers to manage conversation process identifiers directly.
acceptance: ConversationHandle::send<M: Serialize> accepts a typed message and returns Result<(), SdkError>
ConversationHandle::receive<M: Deserialize> returns Future<Output = Result<M, SdkError>>
ConversationHandle::lifecycle returns an impl Stream<Item = ConversationEvent>
ConversationEvent enum has variants: Opened, Message, Closing, Closed, Error
Each ConversationEvent variant carries a conversation identifier

requirements.title: Define SchemaValidate trait for compile-time type enforcement
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define a SchemaValidate trait that message types implement to declare their schema. The trait SHALL provide a method returning schema metadata (name, version, and schema bytes). THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT perform runtime schema validation in this trait -- it provides the metadata that the bus uses for publish-time validation per ADR-002.
acceptance: SchemaValidate trait is defined in types.rs with a schema_metadata() method
schema_metadata() returns a struct containing schema name, version, and schema bytes
The trait has a blanket or derive-macro path documented for user types
SchemaValidate is re-exported from the crate root

requirements.title: Enforce type parameter constraints on publish and subscribe
requirements.spec: WHEN a publish method is called, THE SYSTEM SHALL require the type parameter to satisfy both Serialize and SchemaValidate bounds. WHEN a subscribe method is called, THE SYSTEM SHALL require the type parameter to satisfy DeserializeOwned. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT allow untyped publish or subscribe operations.
acceptance: A struct implementing Serialize + SchemaValidate can be published without additional ceremony
A struct implementing only Serialize (not SchemaValidate) cannot be passed to publish -- compile error
Subscribe's type parameter requires DeserializeOwned, not Deserialize<'a> with a lifetime
No method on ChannelHandle accepts raw bytes or untyped payloads

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement concrete channel or conversation types -- only trait definitions.
SHALL NOT implement connection management or transport logic.
SHALL NOT implement backpressure response types (deferred to SDK-003).
SHALL NOT implement embedded or remote mode distinction (deferred to SDK-004).
SHALL NOT add network dependencies to the crate.
verification: cargo check -p agora-sdk compiles cleanly with trait definitions
cargo clippy -p agora-sdk -- -D warnings passes with no diagnostics
cargo fmt -p agora-sdk --check reports no formatting issues
All public traits and types have documentation comments
No file exceeds 500 lines of code

adrs.title: Conversations as the fundamental messaging unit, not messages
adrs.decision: The fundamental unit is the conversation — a supervised beamr process owning a structured exchange between parties — over the message. A conversation mediates lifecycle, failure handling, and (optionally) durability. Exchange patterns (request-reply, streaming, saga) are conversation types with different lifecycle rules, not special-case bolt-ons. The rejected alternative is the NATS/Kafka model: messages as the primitive, patterns assembled from messages.

adrs.title: Typed channels with compile-time enforcement in Gleam and runtime validation for external producers
adrs.decision: Every channel declares its message type. Gleam producers get compile-time enforcement. External producers (Python, TypeScript, HTTP) get runtime validation at publish time. The rejected alternative is the NATS model: channels carry opaque bytes and consumers handle deserialization failures.

checklist.text: ChannelHandle defined in agora_sdk::channel with publish, subscribe, and request_reply methods.
ConversationHandle defined in agora_sdk::conversation with send, receive, and lifecycle observation methods.
SchemaValidate trait defined in agora_sdk::types for compile-time type enforcement on publish and subscribe.

stories.text: As a Rust developer embedding agora, I want to publish typed messages to a channel through an in-process handle so that I get zero-hop messaging without network overhead.
As a Rust developer connecting remotely, I want publish operations to return typed backpressure signals (accept, defer, reject) so that I can implement application-level flow control.

constraints.text: No file over 500 lines. If a module approaches this limit, extract into submodules.
No panics in production code. .unwrap() and .expect() only in tests.
mod.rs contains ONLY pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports. No logic.
All public types implement Debug. All error types implement std::error::Error.
The Rust SDK must compile with no_std + alloc for WASM targets.
Backpressure responses must never be silently consumed by the SDK. Every pressure signal reaches the application.
Reconnection attempts use exponential backoff with jitter. No fixed-interval retry loops.
The TypeScript SDK must work in both browser (WASM) and server (Node.js) environments without conditional imports in application code.

resolved_context.intention:
When this cluster is complete, developers in three ecosystems can connect to agora and use its full feature set — channels, conversations, typed messages, backpressure — with APIs that feel native to each language. The Rust SDK is the primary interface for embedded use: Aion workflows, co-located services, and any Rust application that wants zero-hop messaging. The Gleam SDK is the authoring convenience layer: message types defined in Gleam get compile-time enforcement, and conversation handlers are authored as Gleam modules running in beamr. The TypeScript SDK serves browser WASM and server-side Node.js, bringing agora to web applications and the JavaScript ecosystem.

The experience should be: import the SDK, connect (or embed), and start conversing. Connection management, reconnection, subscription state recovery, and backpressure handling are SDK responsibilities — application code deals with messages and conversations, not transport.
